sections.heading: Proceedings in Scotland.
sections.title: Sheriff jurisdiction for computer misuse offences
sections.summary: A sheriff in Scotland may exercise jurisdiction over computer misuse offences if the accused was within the sheriffdom at the time of the unlawful act or if a computer containing unauthorized program or data was within the sheriffdom at that time. The section also specifies jurisdiction for offences under section 3ZA and 3A, including conditions related to the accused's actions and computer location. Jurisdiction extends to offences committed outside Scotland where the accused is apprehended or in custody in a Scottish sheriff court district.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines jurisdictional conditions for computer misuse offences in Scotland, relevant for cross-border data processing where the computer or accused is located in Scotland. It impacts DTIAs by establishing geographical scope for jurisdiction over computer-related offences.

requirements.text: Sheriff has jurisdiction for computer misuse offences if accused was in sheriffdom at time of unlawful act
Sheriff has jurisdiction if computer containing unauthorized program or data was in sheriffdom at time of act
Sheriff has jurisdiction for offences under section 3ZA if accused was in sheriffdom at time of act
Sheriff has jurisdiction for offences under section 3A if computer was in sheriffdom at time of act
Offence committed outside Scotland may be prosecuted in Scottish sheriff court district where accused is apprehended
Offence committed outside Scotland may be prosecuted in Scottish sheriff court district directed by Lord Advocate

sections.source_quote: A sheriff shall have jurisdiction in respect of an offence under section 1 or 2 above if— (a) the accused was in the sheriffdom at the time when he did the act which caused the computer to perform the function; or [F60(b) any computer containing any program or data to which the accused by doing that act secured or intended to secure unauthorised access, or enabled or intended to enable unauthorised access to be secured, was in the sheriffdom at that time.]
sections.relevance_reason: jurisdiction for computer misuse offences in Scotland

sections.heading: Interpretation.
sections.title: Definition of unauthorized computer access
sections.summary: This section defines what constitutes unauthorized access to computer programs or data, including conditions for altering, copying, moving, using, or outputting data. It specifies that unauthorized access occurs when a person is not entitled to control access or lacks consent from someone entitled to control access. The section also clarifies that access to removable storage media within a computer is included and that a series of acts constitutes an 'act' for jurisdiction purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides critical definitions for unauthorized access to computer data, directly relevant to DTIAs by establishing what constitutes unauthorized access and how it applies to data transfers across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: A person secures access to program or data if they alter, erase, copy, move, use, or output it
Using a program requires causing it to be executed or being a function of the program
Output of program instructions is immaterial as long as they can be executed or processed
Access is unauthorized if person is not entitled to control access or lacks consent from entitled person
References to program include parts of a program

sections.source_quote: A person secures access to any program or data held in a computer if by causing a computer to perform any function he— (a) alters or erases the program or data; (b) copies or moves it to any storage medium other than that in which it is held or to a different location in the storage medium in which it is held; (c) uses it; or (d) has it output from the computer in which it is held (whether by having it displayed or in any other manner);
sections.relevance_reason: Defines unauthorized access for data compliance
